description:
You are an integration-developer who has just been asked to add a light-weight “sanity” monitoring step to the build pipeline.  
From the running Linux shell, collect a snapshot of the host’s basic health indicators (CPU load, memory, disk and network) and archive them in a very specific, **machine-parsable** way so that the CI system can pick the data up automatically.

Target directory (must be created if it does not yet exist)  
/home/user/system_diagnostics

When you finish, exactly **three** new files have to be present inside that directory:

1. /home/user/system_diagnostics/diagnostics.log  
   • Plain UTF-8 text.  
   • Must consist of five consecutive sections, each beginning with a marker line that contains three equals signs, one space, an UPPER-CASE identifier, one space, and three equals signs.  
     Required section identifiers (in this order):  
       UPTIME, CPU, MEMORY, DISK, NETWORK  
   • Between two marker lines place the command output that generated that section; do **not** add any extra text.  
     The recommended commands are:  
       uptime ‑p or uptime;  
       top -b -n 1 | head -5 OR lscpu;  
       free -m;  
       df -h;  
       ip -4 addr show OR ip a.  
   • Example skeleton (your real outputs will differ):  
     === UPTIME ===  
     14:27:11 up  2:10,  1 user,  load average: 0.01, 0.03, 0.00  
     === CPU ===  
     …  
     === MEMORY ===  
     …  
     === DISK ===  
     …  
     === NETWORK ===  
     …

2. /home/user/system_diagnostics/memory_summary.csv  
   • CSV with **no header row** and exactly one record.  
   • The record must have three integer columns in this order (no thousands separator, no unit):  
       total_mb,used_mb,free_mb  
   • Example of the required layout (values shown only as an illustration):  
     7829,1134,6124

3. /home/user/system_diagnostics/disk_root_usage.txt  
   • A one-line plain-text file containing only the numeric usage percentage (without the “%”) of the root filesystem “/”.  
   • Example if root is 37 % full:  
     37

Important additional requirements:

• Do **not** write anything to standard output other than the normal command output you use while working – the verification harness does not parse your conversation.  
• Make sure every file ends with a single newline character (Unix line ending).  
• Use only user-writable locations; you do not need sudo.  
• When you are done, the three files must exist exactly as specified so the automated judge can read them verbatim.  
• No other files are expected in /home/user/system_diagnostics.

The CI system’s automated test will validate:
a) the presence of the three files at the exact paths given above;  
b) that diagnostics.log contains the five required marker lines in the correct order;  
c) that memory_summary.csv contains three comma-separated integers in a single line;  
d) that disk_root_usage.txt contains a single integer without additional characters.

Proceed to the terminal and perform the necessary steps to satisfy all of the above. When everything looks good, you may consider the task finished.
truth:
Pre-existing state before the agent starts:

• The directory /home/user does exist and is writable.  
• The directory /home/user/system_diagnostics does NOT yet exist.

What the grader will check after the agent finishes:

1. File existence  
   - /home/user/system_diagnostics/diagnostics.log  
   - /home/user/system_diagnostics/memory_summary.csv  
   - /home/user/system_diagnostics/disk_root_usage.txt

2. diagnostics.log content rules  
   a) Contains these five exact marker lines **in this order** (case-sensitive, no extra spaces):  
      "=== UPTIME ==="  
      "=== CPU ==="  
      "=== MEMORY ==="  
      "=== DISK ==="  
      "=== NETWORK ==="  
   b) There is at least one non-empty line between every two marker lines.

3. memory_summary.csv rules  
   a) The file has exactly one line.  
   b) That line matches the regex: ^[0-9]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+\n$

4. disk_root_usage.txt rules  
   a) The file has exactly one line.  
   b) That line matches the regex: ^[0-9]+\n$

No validation of the numeric correctness of the collected statistics is performed; only formatting and presence are checked.
